output:
**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can promoting PlainMamba's fixed content-agnostic Continuous 2D scan order to a learned input-adaptive token ordering, which provably reduces to Continuous-2D-Scan, ViM, and VMamba scans under stated parameter limits, beat the fixed scan on visual recognition at matched parameter and FLOP budget?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

Plain, non-hierarchical, constant-resolution encoders - the ViT-like substrate PlainMamba targets - are the most broadly reused building block in modern vision. SAM, DINOv2, the CLIP image encoder, the encoders feeding LLaVA-style multimodal models, and the image backbones behind text-conditional generation systems are all plain encoders chosen precisely because they are simple, uniform, and easy to pretrain and transfer. Their well-known cost is that a plain backbone usually trails a hierarchical one at matched budget. A cheap, drop-in adaptive scan that lifts a plain selective-scan backbone toward hierarchical accuracy without adding parameters or FLOPs is therefore high-leverage: because the change lives inside the scan and leaves the rest of the architecture untouched, any gain transfers directly to downstream detection, segmentation, and multimodal encoders that already standardize on plain backbones. The improvement compounds wherever the substrate is reused.

For the research community, a result here reframes scan design from a hand-tuned hyperparameter - the regime of ViM, VMamba, PlainMamba, and LocalMamba's offline search - into a learnable, content-adaptive component of the model. That is a conceptual shift: instead of asking which fixed traversal is best on average, the field can ask what order this image should induce, and let training answer. It also provides a transferable template for adapting inherently 1D sequence models (S4, S6, Mamba) to 2D and other structured data: when a 1D recurrence with memory decay must consume a non-1D signal, the ordering that defines scan-distance can be made a first-class learned object rather than a fixed serialization. Just as importantly, the boundary-reduction methodology (provable collapse to named prior scans under stated parameter limits) paired with the Frozen-Random-Permutation placebo is a reusable evaluation discipline; future work proposing any adaptive routing, ordering, or sparsity mechanism can adopt it to prove that gains come from learned adaptivity rather than added capacity.

In terms of knowledge advanced and practical applications, the payoff is better accuracy-per-FLOP for linear-time vision backbones relative to quadratic-attention ViTs. Selective-scan SSMs are linear in sequence length, which is exactly where they matter most: high-resolution inputs and dense-prediction tasks (detection, segmentation) where attention's quadratic cost is punishing, and resource-constrained or edge settings where FLOP budgets are tight. Demonstrating that a learned, per-image scan order extracts more recognition accuracy from the same budget makes linear-time backbones more competitive precisely in the regimes that motivate them, and does so through a component small and modular enough to retrofit into existing Mamba-family encoders.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

This problem is hard for five concrete, separable reasons, and each has a known mitigation that keeps the work tractable rather than speculative.

First, the core operation is non-differentiable. Turning image content into a token order requires an argsort or a discrete permutation, and argsort has no useful gradient, so we cannot backpropagate task loss into "which token comes next" naively. We mitigate this with a differentiable relaxation: a Gumbel-Sinkhorn head that emits a soft, doubly-stochastic matrix (a relaxed permutation) during training, annealed toward a hard permutation, and discretized by Hungarian or greedy argmax at inference. This introduces a train-test gap risk - the soft order the network trains on is not exactly the hard order it runs on - which we control with temperature annealing, a straight-through estimator on the hardened order, and an explicit measurement of the soft-versus-hard accuracy gap as an ablation.

Second, and most important, is the linear-time tension. PlainMamba's entire value proposition is an O(N) selective scan instead of O(N^2) attention. A genuinely global learned permutation over N tokens is itself O(N^2) to compute (all-pairs ordering, or a full Sinkhorn over N), which would erase Mamba's efficiency advantage and make the comparison dishonest. This is the central engineering constraint: adaptivity must be bought without quadratic cost. We resolve it by never computing a global all-pairs order. The default mechanism only blends a constant number of precomputed fixed orders (linear by construction); the higher-ceiling mechanism applies Gumbel-Sinkhorn only inside small local windows (cost O(N w^2) with window size w fixed and small) or builds a sparse k-NN feature graph (k fixed) and traverses it greedily, both of which are linear in N.

Third is matched-budget fairness. The ordering head adds parameters and FLOPs, so a naive accuracy gain could simply be "a slightly bigger model," not "a better idea." Any honest headline claim of better accuracy at the same budget therefore requires that the head be cheap and that its cost be compensated - we shrink backbone width to hold total parameters and FLOPs fixed against PlainMamba within a stated small tolerance (epsilon), and the Frozen-Random-Permutation placebo is built to carry the same head overhead. This is exactly why the placebo and the matched-FLOP and matched-parameter twins are not optional niceties; they are the instruments that distinguish "learned routing helped" from "more capacity helped."

Fourth is the systems cost of per-image orders. When every image induces a different recurrence order, the scan can no longer assume a static contiguous layout; efficient execution needs gather and scatter to permute the token sequence before the scan and unpermute after, batched across images with different permutations. Done carelessly this destroys throughput. We rely on standard fused gather/scatter plus index-select kernels and budget a "do-no-harm" epsilon on throughput and peak memory versus PlainMamba; if the soft-permutation head violates it, the content-routed mixture default (which permutes among a fixed small basis and can precompute index tables) recovers most of the gain at near-zero systems overhead.

Fifth is credit assignment. A measured gain could come from the learned order, from the extra parameters of the head, or simply from exposing the model to more scan directions. A plain "just learn the scan" experiment cannot tell these apart and is therefore not a valid test of the hypothesis. The falsifier ladder is built precisely to separate them: the oracle bounds how much any routing could help; the placebo holds parameters, FLOPs, and content-correlation fixed while removing task-training; the "more-scans" control gives PlainMamba the same number of extra fixed scans, matched in parameters, FLOPs, and passes; and the boundary reductions provide a verified floor. The hypothesis only survives if learned > placebo > fixed-scan at matched budget - a strong, falsifiable bar.

Why it is nonetheless tractable: every hard part maps to an off-the-shelf tool (Gumbel-Sinkhorn, straight-through estimation, fused gather/scatter), the mixture and local-window variants are provably linear, and the boundary reductions guarantee the method can never do worse than PlainMamba, ViM, VMamba, or raster by construction, so the downside is bounded while the upside is what we measure.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

This work is positioned directly against the hand-designed scan lineage that defines vision state-space models, and its contribution is best understood as filling a specific, previously unclaimed quadrant in that design space. The lineage to date has treated the 2D-to-1D traversal as a fixed engineering choice. ViM flattens the grid with a raster order and adds a backward pass (bidirectional raster). VMamba introduces 4-way Cross-Scan, running four fixed axis-aligned raster traversals and merging them. PlainMamba improves the locality of these orders with Continuous 2D Scanning - a fixed serpentine (boustrophedon) traversal so consecutive tokens are spatially adjacent - plus Direction-Aware Updating, a fixed relative-direction bias. LocalMamba is the closest prior in spirit because it does not accept a single global order: it partitions the image into windows and searches over a discrete set of scan directions. But its search is offline and combinatorial over a small candidate set, it selects directions rather than learning a continuous per-image ordering, and the resulting order is not produced end-to-end as a function of each image's content. Related backbones - spatial-graph SSMs, RWKV-style and other Mamba-family vision models, efficiency-oriented VMamba variants, and assorted multi-directional or zig-zag scans - either change the linear-recurrence operator, the hierarchy, or add more fixed scans; none of them make the order itself a learned, per-image variable.

The sharpened novel delta is this: a per-image, content-conditioned, end-to-end-learned token ordering or sparse spatial adjacency that feeds an unchanged selective scan, explicitly controlling which neighbors receive short scan-distance under the recurrence's memory decay. The object being learned is the causal order (equivalently, the induced adjacency or permutation), not the operator and not the token set. This is categorically distinct from four nearby families. (a) Deformable attention and deformable convolution learn where to sample and how to reweight attention; they do not impose a causal sequential order on a recurrent SSM, and they retain quadratic or sampling-based interaction rather than acting through scan-distance under decay. (b) Learned token merging and pruning change the token count; here the count is fixed and only the ordering changes, so capacity and FLOPs are held constant. (c) Graph rewiring in GNNs alters an undirected message-passing topology, not a single causal traversal of a selective-scan recurrence. (d) Sequence-ordering tricks in NLP reorder discrete symbols without a 2D spatial-contiguity objective tied to recurrent memory decay. The unclaimed quadrant is therefore precise: learned (not searched, not hand-designed) + per-image (not a single global order) + ordering-not-weights + ordering-not-count, with provable reductions to the fixed scans. Zero routing logits (or temperature to infinity) recover PlainMamba Continuous-2D bit-identically (B1); restriction of the permutation class to raster forward and reverse recovers ViM (B2); restriction of the router to the four fixed axes with input-independent uniform weights recovers VMamba's Cross-Scan order (B3); and the identity order recovers the raw-raster floor (B4). B3 is an exact reduction along the scan-order axis only: it reproduces VMamba's four traversal orders, while VMamba's separate 4-pass parallel-scan-and-merge is an orthogonal axis that is held fixed rather than reproduced by the ordering module. No prior method simultaneously occupies that quadrant or comes equipped with these exact reductions.

Finally, the central placebo - Frozen-Random-Permutation - is a disentanglement test the prior literature lacks. Every existing comparison conflates several confounds: a new scan typically adds parameters, adds extra scan passes, or simply makes the order input-dependent, and any accuracy gain could be attributed to any of these rather than to genuinely learned adaptivity. The placebo is an identical-parameter, identical-FLOP module that emits a per-image permutation from a fixed random hash of pixel statistics, so it is content-correlated and input-dependent but not task-trained. Requiring that the learned ordering beat the placebo, and that the placebo in turn beat the fixed scan, all at matched budget, cleanly separates learned adaptivity from more parameters, more scans, and mere input-dependence. This control is itself a methodological contribution, and its absence is a large part of why the question has not been answered cleanly before: prior scan proposals had no instrument to show that the order, rather than the added capacity, was doing the work.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

Goal and surgical scope. We change exactly one component of PlainMamba: the scan-order module. The selective-scan core (input-dependent B, C, Delta; recurrence h_i = Abar h_{i-1} + Bbar x_i) is left untouched, so all of Mamba's linear-time properties are inherited unchanged. PlainMamba's fixed Continuous-2D serpentine is promoted to a learned, input-adaptive ordering that decides which neighbors get short scan-distance for this image, then hands the reordered token sequence to the unmodified scan.

Default mechanism - content-routed mixture of fixed scans (the linear, do-no-harm default). We keep a small fixed basis of precomputed orders - serpentine, transpose-serpentine, a generalized or clipped Hilbert (Gilbert) curve that is well-defined for non-square and non-power-of-two grids, and the four VMamba cross-scan axes - and a tiny gating MLP emits per-image, and optionally per-region, soft routing weights over that basis. The block runs the scan under the routed order (soft-blended at training time, top-1 or hardened at inference). This variant is linear by construction, has near-zero systems overhead because index tables are precomputed, trivially admits the boundary reductions B1 through B3, and is the configuration we ship if the higher-ceiling head cannot meet the throughput gate.

Higher-ceiling mechanism - content-conditioned local soft-permutation head. From the block's input tokens we compute lightweight per-token order features with a tiny pointwise projection (a 1x1 conv plus a small MLP). We partition the token grid into fixed local windows of size w x w. Inside each window we run a few Sinkhorn iterations on a learned scoring matrix to produce a soft (doubly-stochastic) permutation, perturbed with Gumbel noise for exploration and annealed by temperature toward a hard assignment. Windows are then stitched along a coarse inter-window serpentine, so global structure is preserved while intra-window order is content-adaptive. Cost is O(N w^2) with w fixed (linear in N). At inference the soft permutation is hardened to a discrete permutation (greedy or Hungarian argmax) and applied by gather before the scan and scatter after. As an alternative realization of this ceiling we build a sparse k-NN graph over the order features (k fixed) and define the scan as a greedy nearest-unvisited traversal (a cheap Hamiltonian-style walk); this is also linear and tends to make spatially and semantically adjacent tokens scan-adjacent.

Direction-Aware Updating generalization. PlainMamba adds a fixed relative-direction bias indexed by a small table of canonical moves (up, down, left, right) between consecutive scan tokens. Because our learned order can place tokens that are not 4-connected next to each other, we replace the fixed table with a learned embedding of the actual relative displacement (dx, dy) between consecutive tokens in the learned order, bucketed into a small set of offset bins. When the order is restricted to the serpentine, only the canonical offsets occur and this exactly recovers PlainMamba's fixed direction table; the relation is now conditioned on the learned adjacency rather than hard-coded.

Boundary reductions (built in, then verified numerically). B1: drive routing logits to zero or temperature to infinity, so the module emits the fixed serpentine and the model is bit-identical to PlainMamba. B2: restrict the permutation class to raster forward and reverse, recovering ViM. B3: restrict the router to the four fixed axes with input-independent uniform weights, recovering VMamba's Cross-Scan order (the scan-order axis only; VMamba's 4-pass parallel merge is a separate axis held fixed). B4: identity order, recovering the raw-raster floor. These are not just claims; they are an ablation row (see Recovery below).

Training and inference setup; budget matching. We drop the ordering module into PlainMamba blocks in place of the fixed-scan module and train end to end with the standard recipe. We instantiate matched twins of PlainMamba's released L1, L2, and L3 variants (exact channel widths, depths, and FLOP counts to be confirmed against the public configurations, since the matched-budget tables depend on them) and report the ordering module's parameter and FLOP overhead explicitly. To keep the headline claim honest - same parameter and FLOP budget, better accuracy - we shrink backbone width (channel count) to absorb the head's cost so total parameters and FLOPs match PlainMamba within a stated small tolerance, and we report both a matched-parameter twin and a matched-FLOP twin. Because FLOP-parity is not latency- or energy-parity (Sinkhorn iterations and gather/scatter can be FLOP-cheap but hardware-inefficient), we also report wall-clock throughput, latency, and peak memory next to FLOPs.

Datasets. ImageNet-1k for classification (primary signal). ADE20K with UperNet for semantic segmentation. COCO with Mask R-CNN for object detection and instance segmentation. Robustness: ImageNet-C, ImageNet-A, and ImageNet-R, since plain, constant-resolution backbones are argued to be robust and adaptive ordering should help on corrupted or shifted inputs.

Baselines. PlainMamba at matched budget (primary). ViM, VMamba, and LocalMamba as SSM vision peers. DeiT (plain ViT) and ConvNeXt at matched budget as attention and convolution references. An SSM-agnostic raster floor (B4) anchors the bottom.

Metrics. Top-1 accuracy (ImageNet-1k); mIoU (ADE20K); box AP and mask AP (COCO); accuracy-per-FLOP and accuracy-per-parameter Pareto curves; throughput (images per second) and peak memory at high input resolution; mean corruption error (mCE) for ImageNet-C and clean-versus-shifted accuracy for -A and -R.

Ablations and falsifier ladder (the decisive part).
- Oracle: for each image pick the best order from the small basis by brute-force evaluation, an upper bound on what any router could buy. The learned head should approach, not exceed, the oracle.
- Placebo (central, in the main table): Frozen-Random-Permutation, a module with identical parameters and FLOPs (the same head overhead as the method) that emits a per-image permutation from a fixed random hash of pixel statistics. It is content-correlated but not task-trained. The required, falsifiable ordering is learned > placebo > fixed-scan, all at matched budget. If learned does not beat placebo, the hypothesis is refuted: the gain was content-correlation or capacity, not learned task-relevant routing.
- Recovery: numerically verify B1 through B4 reproduce PlainMamba, ViM, VMamba (scan-order axis), and raster to within noise, confirming the method strictly generalizes its ancestors.
- Adaptivity probe: quantify how much the learned order varies across images and whether short-scan-distance adjacencies correlate with object boundaries and saliency maps, direct evidence the order actually adapts to content rather than collapsing to a constant.
- Budget control: matched-FLOP and matched-parameter twins, plus a "more-scans" control that gives PlainMamba the same number of extra fixed scans the mixture consumes, matched in parameters, FLOPs, and passes, isolating learned routing from simply more directions.
- Do-no-harm: the method must not regress throughput or peak memory beyond a stated epsilon versus PlainMamba; if the soft-permutation head fails this, the content-routed mixture is the shipped configuration.

Expected outcomes. We expect the learned ordering to beat the fixed Continuous-2D scan at matched parameter and FLOP budget on ImageNet-1k top-1, with the gain carrying over to ADE20K mIoU and COCO box/mask AP because the change lives in the reusable backbone; we expect the largest relative gains at higher input resolution and on dense-prediction tasks, where scan-distance under memory decay matters most. The decisive signal is the strict ordering learned > placebo > fixed-scan in the single main table (learned, placebo, oracle, PlainMamba, and the more-scans control, all at matched parameters and FLOPs), with the learned head landing between the placebo and the oracle. The adaptivity probe should show the learned order varying across images and correlating with object boundaries, and the Recovery row should reproduce the ancestor scans. The proposal fails cleanly, and we report it as such, if (a) the placebo matches or beats the learned ordering, or (b) the learned ordering cannot beat the fixed scan at matched budget, or (c) meeting the do-no-harm throughput and memory gate is only possible at an accuracy that no longer beats PlainMamba. This single matched-budget table answers Question 1 directly and cannot be confounded by capacity or by extra directions.

---

**Limitations and Responsible-AI Notes**

This is an academic architecture proposal; no models, datasets, or weights are released, and the method changes only the token ordering fed to an unchanged selective scan, so it adds efficiency rather than a new capability. Stated gains over the fixed scan, accuracy-per-FLOP advantages, and transfer to SAM, DINOv2, CLIP, LLaVA-style, and text-to-image encoders are conjectured pending experiments and are marked to verify; matched parameter and FLOP budget does not imply matched wall-clock, latency, or memory, since Sinkhorn and k-NN gather/scatter can be FLOP-cheap but hardware-inefficient, so we report throughput, latency, and peak memory alongside FLOPs. Because the scan order is content-conditioned, it can change the failure distribution and not only the mean; trained on ImageNet, COCO, and ADE20K (with known geographic and object-frequency skew) it may favor canonical content and regress on tail or atypical inputs, so we report per-class, tail-class, and worst-group deltas plus ImageNet-C/-A/-R robustness rather than averages alone. As a general-purpose and cheaper vision backbone, improvements are dual-use, including surveillance and biometric identification at the edge; we frame the contribution as capability-neutral efficiency and avoid optimizing for identification benchmarks. To limit compute and carbon we run the placebo-gated falsifier ladder at small scale before full training, reuse public pretrained baselines where possible, report GPU-hours and estimated CO2e, and release code, configs, seeds, and at least 3-seed mean and standard deviation results for both the method and the Frozen-Random-Permutation placebo for reproducibility.
